title: Parallel Recording

content.question: What's in your head that should be in a system?
sop: Capture every task, idea, or commitment the moment it enters your head, one inbox, not five
Rate your current cognitive load and count how many external aids you're actually using (see calculation, page 5)
Review the capture inbox daily and route each item, do it now, schedule it, or file it
Never let an item live only in your memory once it's been captured once
Trust the system enough to stop mentally rehearsing what's already written down
content.explainer: Your working memory isn't built to hold open commitments, every uncaptured task stays partially active in the background, quietly draining attention even when you're not consciously thinking about it. Writing it down once doesn't just prevent forgetting; it closes the loop your brain was still running.
questions: What's in your head that should be in a system?
What did you almost forget this week that would've been fine if it lived in a system instead of your memory?
How many open loops are you currently holding in your head that a five-minute capture session would close?
content.equation_example: Tasks: 20 open items you're mentally tracking (count). External Aids: 15 of them are captured in a system. Cognitive Load = 20 minus 15 = 5 items still living in your head. Lower cognitive load means more mental bandwidth for actual thinking; a high number means you're spending attention on remembering instead of doing.
content.closing_statement: Anything captured once stops costing you attention twice.

caption:
Principle Nº 223: Parallel Recording

External memory aids. Your working memory isn't built to hold open commitments, every uncaptured task stays partially active in the background, quietly draining attention even when you're not consciously thinking about it.

Ask yourself: What's in your head that should be in a system?
